coredata.dc:title: Down/up-conversion dual-mode ratiometric fluorescence imprinted sensor embedded with metal-organic frameworks for dual-channel multi-emission multiplexed visual detection of thiamphenicol

coredata.dc:description: The establishment of a fluorescence sensing system for sensitive and selective visual detection of trace antibiotics is of great significance to food safety and human health risk assessment. A simple and rapid one-pot strategy was developed successfully to synthesize a down/up-conversion dual-excitation multi-emission fluorescence imprinted sensor for dual-channel thiamphenicol (TAP) detection. In this strategy, the metal-organic frameworks were in situ incorporated into the fluorescence imprinted sensor, guiding the coordination induced emission of abiotic carbon dots and signal-amplification effect of fluorescence sensing. Under dual-excitation (370 nm and 780 nm), the fluorescence imprinted sensor exhibited a dual-channel fluorescence response toward TAP with two-part linear ranges of 5.0 nM–6.0 μM and 6.0 μM–26.0 μM. Significantly, the fluorescence color ranged from blue to purple to red can be observed with the naked eye. The results of the dual-channel TAP determination in actual samples by the fluorescence imprinted sensor indicated that the fluorescence imprinted sensor provided a sensitive, selective, and multiplexed visual detection of TAP in complex sample.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Fluorescence emission spectra excited a 370 nm of bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP prepared with different ratios of aCDs and bCDs (a: 2 mL aCDs + 2 mL bCDs; b: 2 mL aCDs + 5 mL bCDs; c: 2 mL aCDs + 8 mL bCDs) and fluorescence color changed with the addition of TAP (5.0 μM and 20.0 μM) under UV irradiation at 365 nm. (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP (a), bCDs (b), aCDs (c), aCDs@ZIF-8 (d) and ZIF-8 (e) excited at 370 nm (inset exhibited the corresponding digital images under UV irradiation at 365 nm. (C) Fluorescence emission spectra of bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP (a), bCDs (b), aCDs (c), aCDs@ZIF-8 (d) and ZIF-8 (e) excited at 780 nm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(A–C) TEM and (D–F) SEM images of bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP, bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8 and bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@MIP, respectively. (G) Elemental mappings of different elements (C, N, O, Si, Zn) and EDS spectra recorded from bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP. (H) FT-IR spectra of bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP (a), bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@NIP (b), ZIF-8 (c) and bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@MIP (d). (I) XRD patterns of bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP, bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@MIP and ZIF-8.

(A and B) Fluorescence emission spectra of bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP at different down-conversion excitation wavelengths (370, 380, 390, 400 and 410 nm) and up-conversion excitation wavelengths (760, 770, 780, 790,800, 810 and 820 nm), respectively. (C and D) Fluorescence emission spectra and response efficiency (inset) of bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP under dual-channel excitation of 370 nm and 780 nm at different pHs (4.0–11.0). (E and F) Fluorescence quenching efficiency of bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP and bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@NIP toward TAP (5.0 μM) at different time (0–5 min) under 370 nm and 780 nm excitation.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP and (B) Calibration plot and calibration equation of bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP and bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@NIP toward TAP (5.0 nM- 26.0 μM) under down-conversion excitation channel (370 nm). (C) Fluorescence emission spectra and (D) quenching efficiency ((F456/F680)0/(F456/F680)]) of bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@MIP toward TAP (5.0 nM- 26.0 μM) under down-conversion excitation channel (370 nm). (E) Fluorescence emission spectra of bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP and (F) corresponding quenching efficiency ((F460 × F523/F682)0/(F456 × F523/F682)]) toward TAP (5.0 nM- 26.0 μM) under up-conversion excitation channel (780 nm). The photography images in the A are the fluorescence color change (clockwise) with the addition of TAP (5.0 nM- 26.0 μM) under UV irradiation at 365 nm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

The fluorescence quenching efficiency excited at 370 nm (A) and 780 nm (B) of bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP and bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@NIP toward TAP (5.0 μM) and its analogs. (Inset A and B: Fluorescence quenching efficiency of bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP with addition of TAP and other four analogs in the concentration ranges from 5.0 nM to 26.0 μM. The photography images in the A are the fluorescence color change by TAP (5.0 μM) and analogs (right to left) under UV irradiation at 365 nm). The effect of coexisting interferents (5.0 μM) on the fluorescence quenching efficiency excited at 370 nm (C) and 790 nm (D) of bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP and bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@NIP toward TAP (5.0 μM). (E and F) Dual-excitation channel fluorescence emission spectra of fluorescence dye, bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP and bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP interfered by fluorescence dye. head.ce:title: Down/up-conversion dual-mode ratiometric fluorescence imprinted sensor embedded with metal-organic frameworks for dual-channel multi-emission multiplexed visual detection of thiamphenicol

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The establishment of a fluorescence sensing system for sensitive and selective visual detection of trace antibiotics is of great significance to food safety and human health risk assessment. A simple and rapid one-pot strategy was developed successfully to synthesize a down/up-conversion dual-excitation multi-emission fluorescence imprinted sensor for dual-channel thiamphenicol (TAP) detection. In this strategy, the metal-organic frameworks were in situ incorporated into the fluorescence imprinted sensor, guiding the coordination induced emission of abiotic carbon dots and signal-amplification effect of fluorescence sensing. Under dual-excitation (370 nm and 780 nm), the fluorescence imprinted sensor exhibited a dual-channel fluorescence response toward TAP with two-part linear ranges of 5.0 nM–6.0 μM and 6.0 μM–26.0 μM. Significantly, the fluorescence color ranged from blue to purple to red can be observed with the naked eye. The results of the dual-channel TAP determination in actual samples by the fluorescence imprinted sensor indicated that the fluorescence imprinted sensor provided a sensitive, selective, and multiplexed visual detection of TAP in complex sample.

ce:section.ce:para: Thiamphenicol (TAP), one of the broad-spectrum bacteriostatic antibiotics, is prescribed to treat respiratory infections, bacterial prostatitis and sexually transmitted diseases (Liu et al., 2018). However, the residues of TAP in animal food products such as milk, fish and shrimp can enter the human body, causing the series of diseases including hypersensitivity, gastrointestinal erosions, ulcers, and kidney necrosis (Muhammad et al., 2018). The European Community has set the maximum residue limit of TAP in animal-derived foods at 50 μg kg−1 (Guo et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2018). Now, the detection of TAP has attracted extensive attention. Many methods involving gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (Nagata and Oka, 1996), high performance liquid chromatography (Patyra and Kwiatek, 2019), electrochemical method (Muhammad et al., 2018), ultraviolet spectral analysis (Martins and De Oliveira, 2019) and fluorescence sensing (Sa-nguanprang et al., 2022) have been developed for determination TAP.

Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs), as the antibody-like artificial materials with tailor-made binding sites, have been used for selective recognition of target analytes due to their complementary shape, size and functional groups (Arabi et al., 2021a, 2020a). Fluorescence-based molecularly imprinted polymers (FL-MIPs), as a novel fluorescence sensing artificial materials, highlighted the characteristics of rapid, sensitive and selective fluorescence detection (Arabi et al., 2020b; Cai et al., 2019; Chen et al., 2016; Kumar and Kim, 2022). Currently, ratiometric FL-MIPs have attracted attention due to the self-calibration and visualization of fluorescence color changes (Amjadi and Jalili, 2017). However, most ratiometric fluorescence sensing systems were based on down-conversion fluorescence, which is susceptible to high-background signals, resulting in the lacking of sensitivity and accuracy (Liu et al., 2018). Interestingly, Tian et al. prepared the near-infrared excitation ratiometric FL-MIPs for fluorescence detecting perfluorooctane sulfonate (Tian et al., 2021). However, the accuracy of single-channel detection tends to decrease due to the emission spectrum overlap and signal cross-talk (Kurt et al., 2016).

Stokes and anti-stokes type dual-channel emission under down/up-conversion dual-excitation exposed unique advantages in fluorescence sensing, such as the overlapping avoidance of excitation and emission spectra, non-invasiveness of complex samples and accuracy of parallel detection (Cui et al., 2014; Lu et al., 2019). As a water-soluble, low biotoxicity and highly photostable fluorescence nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) possessed unique down/up-conversion dual-channel emission, which was superior to other fluorescence materials involving hydrophobic up-conversion lanthanide-doped rare-earth nanoparticles, quantum dots and organic dyes with potential toxicity and environmental hazards (Cui et al., 2014; Jia et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2022; Wolfbeis, 2015). Moreover, it is a fact that the introduction of CDs into metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), which are composed of inorganic metal ions and organic ligands assembled by coordination bonds, is an effective strategy to prevent the self-quenching of CDs caused by aggregation and enhance fluorescence sensing performance(Ashrafzadeh Afshar et al., 2022; Shu et al., 2021; Wu et al., 2020). As we all known, the down/up-conversion multi-emission ratiometric FL-MIPs sensor for dual-channel visible fluorescence sensing has never been reported.

Herein, a novel dual-mode CD ratiometric FL-MIPs sensor with down/up-conversion dual-excitation multi-emission was proposed to achieve accurate, sensitive and selective visual analysis of TAP. In this strategy, MOFs-based ratiometric FL-MIPs improved the sensitivity and selectivity of TAP detection in complex sample, achieving the visualization of fluorescence color changes. The down/up-conversion multi-emission fluorescence avoids the potential background interference in actual sample, providing accurate analysis result through parallel synchronous dual-channel excitation detection.

Thiamphenicol (TAP, 99.0%) and chloramphenicol (CAP, 99.0%), sulfamerazine (SMZ, 99.0%), sulfadimethoxine (SDM, 99.0%), sulfadiazine (SDZ, 99.0%), zinc nitrate hexahydrate (99.0%), Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, 2-methylimidazole (2-MI, 99.0%), 3-aminopropyltriethoxy silane (APTES, 98.0%), tetraethoxysilane (TEOS, 98.0%), ammonium hydroxide (NH3∙H2O, 25.0%), citrate (CA, 99.5%), ethylenediamine (EDA, 99.0%), and anhydrous ethanol were supplied by Changsha Chemical Reagent Co. (Changsha, China). Chlorinated salts (K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, and Fe3+) were purchased from Aladdin Agent Co. (Shanghai, China). Fresh coriander was purchased from the local supermarket.

Fluorescence intensity was measured using an F-7000 fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan). The morphological images were produced by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEOL JEM 2100F, Japan) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (Carl Zeiss AG Gemini 500, Germany). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained using a Nicolet iS10 FT-IR spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, USA). Ultraviolet–visible absorption spectra were collected using UV-1780 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). X-ray diffraction (XRD) was collected using TD3500 (Dandong Tongda, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of abiotic CDs and biomass CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The abiotic CDs (aCDs) were synthesized according to the reported method with some modifications (Vallan et al., 2018). Briefly, 10 mL of deionized water containing 0.5 g of CA and 0.5 mL of EDA was transferred to a Teflon autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 5 h. After cooling to room temperature, the obtained aCDs were stored at 4 °C prior to use.

The biomass CDs (bCDs) were synthesized according to the previous method with some modifications (Li et al., 2017). Firstly, fresh coriander was cut into small pieces and soaked in the ethanol/water (4:1, v/v) solution for 1 h. Then the bCDs were collected with a 0.22 μm nylon filtration membrane and stored at 4 °C for the following experiments.

Firstly, the solution containing 5.0 mL of bCDs, 50 μL of TEOS and 50 μL of NH3∙H2O were magnetic stirred (300 rpm) at room temperature for 5 h. Then, 2.0 mL of aCDs, 10.0 mL of ultrapure water, 0.25 g of Zn(NO3)2·6H2O and 0.5 g of 2-methylimidazole were added into the above solution and stirred for another 30 min. Next, 100 μL of TEOS and 100 μL of NH3∙H2O, 5.0 mL of ethanol, 10.0 mg of TAP and 100 μL of APTES were added to the above reaction system. After the one-pot self-polymerization reaction was completed under constant stirring (300 rpm) at room temperature for 6 h, TAP was removed by repeatedly ultrasonic washing with methanol/acetic acid mixture (9:1, v/v) until no TAP could be detected by UV–Vis in the supernatant solution. After that, the bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP was further washed with ethanol to remove residual acetic acid until pH was close to neutral. After dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C for 10 h, the bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP was stored at room temperature for further use. The non-imprinted polymer (bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@NIP) was prepared identically except that the template (TAP) was omitted.

Concentrations determination of TAP standard or sample solutions was performed by an accumulative procedure using the bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP or bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@NIP aqueous solution (1.0 mg mL−1, pH = 7.0). After incubating at room temperature for 2 min, the fluorescence intensity was measured respectively using a down/up-conversion dual-excitation channel excited at 370 nm and 780 nm. Every determination was repeated three times and the average value was used. Condition of down-conversion channel is the excitation wavelength of 370 nm with the excitation and emission slit widths of 10.0 nm and photomultiplier tube voltage of 550 V. Condition of up-conversion channel is the excitation wavelength of 780 nm with the excitation and emission slit widths of 10.0 nm and photomultiplier tube voltage of 700 V.

The actual fish, shrimp, beef and milk samples were brought from the local market. Actual meat samples were pretreated using the previous method with some modifications (Cai et al., 2019). Briefly, 10 mL of acetonitrile was mixed with 1.0 g of skinless muscle tissue homogenate of meat under ultrasound for 30 min. After centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 5 min, the supernatant was filtered (0.22 μm) and diluted with ultrapure water to 20.0 mL for fluorescence detection. For the milk samples, the pretreatment procedure was implemented according to a reported method (Chullasat et al., 2018). Firstly, 10.0 mL of milk was centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 10 min to remove fat. Then, the defatted milk was mixed with 20.0 mL of acetonitrile and continued centrifugation at 6000 rpm for 10 min to remove proteins. Finally, the supernatant was reserved for fluorescence detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Validation by high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC)
ce:section.ce:para: Verification experiments were performed using high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) system. The condition of HPLC system involved chromatography pump (LC-20AR, Shimazin, Japan), injection volume (10 μL), diode array detector (SPD-M20A, Shimazin, Japan), and detection wavelength (225 nm). The separation was performed on a C18 reverse-phase chromatographic column (5 μm, 20 × 250 mm, Shimadzu, Japan). The mobile phase was ethanol phosphate/water (7:3, v/v) at a flow rate of 0.8 mL min−1 and was filtered by a 0.45 μm nylon microporous membrane before use.

As shown in Scheme 1 , a novel dual-mode CDs MOF-based fluorescence imprinted sensor (bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP) with down/up-conversion dual-excitation channel multi-emission fluorescence performance was one-pot synthesized for the synchronous parallel multiplex detection of TAP. Firstly, bCDs and aCDs with down/up-conversion fluorescence properties were firstly prepared. Then the bCDs and aCDs were respectively coated with silicon and anchored with ZIF-8. Finally, the imprinted layer was coated on the surface of fluorescence nanomaterial by the one-pot sol-gel method to obtain a sensitive and selective down/up-conversion dual-excitation channel multi-emission fluorescence sensor. In the bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP preparation procedure, SiO2 nanospheres were used as carrier to coat with bCDs (bCDs@SiO2), protecting and stabilizing the fluorescence performance of bCDs (Wang et al., 2016). The in situ incorporation of aCDs in ZIF-8 (aCDs@ZIF-8) improved the fluorescence performance of aCDs due to the anti-aggregation anchoring of ZIF-8, which enhanced the fluorescence sensing sensitivity of aCDs via the signal-amplification effect of ZIF-8 (Shi et al., 2021; Shu et al., 2021). The imprinted layer was occurred on the surface of the mixture of bCDs@SiO2 and aCDs@ZIF-8 through the condensation between APTES and TEOS catalyzed by aqueous ammonia solution in the presence of TAP template. After removing TAP, the empty imprinted cavities in the bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP were obtained, supporting the specific recognition for TAP in complex sample.

In order to realize the diversification of fluorescence color change in TAP visual detection, series of bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP fabricated by different ratios of aCDs and bCDs were prepared. Fig. 1 A shows the maximum emission peak intensity (457 nm and 680 nm) of bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP synthesized by different ratio of aCDs and bCDs under the down-conversion excitation channel (370 nm) and fluorescence color under UV lamp at 365 nm (inset a, b and c). After the addition of TAP (5.0 μM and 20.0 μM), the bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP (2 mL aCDs + 5 mL bCDs) showed a significant fluorescence color change from blue to purple to red under UV lamp at 365 nm (route b), with a richer color variation than other bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP synthesized by different ratio of aCDs and bCDs (route a and c). Up-conversion excitation fluorescence emission was shown in Fig. S1. However, a weak fluorescence color change was observed due to the low up-conversion fluorescence luminescence efficiency (Wolfbeis, 2015; Zhu et al., 2015).

The fluorescence emission spectra of bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP, bCDs, aCDs, aCDs@ZIF-8 and ZIF-8 under dual-excitation channels (370 nm and 780 nm) were studied in detail. According to the previous reports (Zhu et al., 2015), down-conversion channel fluorescence was resulted from the CO, and up-conversion fluorescence was derived from two-photon induced photochemical reactions caused by N-containing heterocyclic ligands (Liu et al., 2019; Medishetty et al., 2017; Yu et al., 2015). As shown in Fig. 1B. The bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP sensor showed the dual-emission at 457 nm and 680 nm (curve a) excited at 370 nm, exhibiting two distinct emissions blue-shifted (from 480 nm to 457 nm, from 690 nm to 680 nm) compared with bCDs (curve b) and aCDs (curve c). The blue-shifted was attributed to the coordination-induced between CDs and 2-MIM dual-ligands and zinc ions in the formation of LMOF and attachment of silica imprinted layer (Fu et al., 2022; Xu et al., 2019; Zhou et al., 2014). The aCDs with a rigid network structure of short polyamide chains were twisted to adapt to the LMOF rigid structure, resulting in the blue-shifted, which is consistent with the coordination-induced emission (CIE) reported in previous reports (Vallan et al., 2018; Yin et al., 2019). In addition, the aCDs@ZIF-8 exhibited a single emission at 450 nm (curve d), which was different from aCDs emission at 480 nm (curve c) and weak emission of the ZIF-8 at 430 nm (curve e), further demonstrating the blue-shifted induced by the coordination-induced effect.

The fluorescence emission of up-conversion excitation channel excited at 780 nm was shown in Fig. 1C. The bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP appeared the triple-emission at 460 nm, 522 nm and 690 nm (curve a), caused by the introduction of bCDs (curve b), aCDs (curve c) and ZIF-8 (curve e). During the synthesis of bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP, the emission of bCDs was blue-shifted (from 700 nm to 682 nm) due to the silica layer attachment (Dai et al., 2015; Zhou et al., 2014). While the aCD@ZIF-8 (curve d) not only was blue-shifted in the up-conversion excitation channel but also enhanced the up-conversion fluorescence intensity due to the coordination-induced effect of aCDs and ZIF-8 (Yin et al., 2019). Above results indicated that the synthesized bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP sensor could be used for synchronous parallel down/up-conversion dual-channel fluorescence determination of TAP.

The interior and surface morphology of bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP, bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8 and bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@MIP was characterized by TEM and SEM in detail. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP with an average size of 250 nm showed a significant core-shell structure coated with about 50 nm imprinted layer. The internal core structure was formed by the incorporating bCDs@SiO2 and aCDs@ZIF-8 (TEM image in Fig. 2B). In comparison, the bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@MIP without ZIF-8 doping exhibited a neat nanosphere structure without core-shell (TEM image in Fig. 2C). In the SEM image, the bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP (shown in Fig. 2D) exhibited a rough imprinted layer compared with smooth bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8 (Fig. 2E), and the smooth spherical bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@MIP (Fig. 2F). In addition, the element mappings of bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP in Fig. 2G showed that C, N, O, Si and Zn were equally distributed in the nanostructure, further confirming the EDS spectra analysis, in which the weight percentage of C, N, O, Si and Zn were 66%, 5%, 15%, 12% and 3%, respectively.

Fig. 2H shows the FT-IR spectra of bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP (curve a), bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@NIP (curve b), ZIF-8 (curve c) and bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8 (curve d). The bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP and bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@NIP exhibited the similarity of the major chemical functional groups. The peaks present at 3144 cm−1, 2918 cm−1 and 3200- 3500 cm−1 were caused by stretching vibration of C–H and N–H/O–H in the aromatic and aliphatic groups. Stretching vibration of CO at 1640 cm−1 mainly comes from CDs and peaks centered at 1415 cm−1 and 990 cm−1 derived from the tensile vibration and plane bending vibration of imidazole rings. Notably, the typical asymmetric Si–O–Si stretching strong absorption peak at 1046 cm−1 appeared in the above materials except ZIF-8, indicating the silica layer produced by the sol-gel polymerization reaction. Moreover, the bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP showed the same absorption peak as ZIF-8 at 750 cm−1 (Zn–O) and 422 cm−1 (Zn–N). The data proved that the bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP was successfully synthesized following the predetermined strategy.

As shown in Fig. 2I, crystal structures of bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP, ZIF-8 and bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@MIP were determined by X-ray diffraction. The bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP showed the characteristic diffraction peaks of ZIF-8, but the intensity decreased significantly, which was caused by the formation of MIP film on the ZIF-8 surface (Cao et al., 2021). In addition, ultra-small size CDs were not enough to break the rigid structure of ZIF-8 during the one-pot synthesizing bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP (Vallan et al., 2018; Yin et al., 2019). For comparison, only one broad characteristic diffraction peak (2θ = 23°) of amorphous SiO2 was observed in the bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@MIP without ZIF-8 doping, which was similar to those previous reports (Chen et al., 2021; Li et al., 2016). Thus, the above results further indicated the successful synthesis of bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP.

The conditions affecting the fluorescence response performance of bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP toward TAP, including excitation wavelength, pH and response time, were optimized. As shown in Fig. 3 A, under the down-conversion excitation channel, the bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP showed the dual-emission at 457 nm and 680 nm, in which the fluorescence intensity from aCDs decreased significantly. In comparison, the fluorescence intensity from bCDs fluctuated slightly with the increment of excitation wavelength (370–410 nm). Under the up-conversion excitation channel (Fig. 3B), the bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP showed the triple-emission fluorescence (460 nm, 522 nm and 682 nm). With the increase of excitation wavelength (760 nm–820 nm), the wavelength-dependent behavior of red-shifted was occurred (Xu et al., 2021). Thus, based on the above data, 370 nm and 780 nm were selected as the best excitation wavelength for the down/up-conversion excitation channel.

Different solution pHs may affect the binding of the imprinting site in the silica layer toward TAP due to the changing the state of functional groups (C.-X. Liu et al., 2020). As shown in Fig. 3C and D, under down/up-conversion excitation channel, the initial fluorescence intensities of bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP were best at pH 7.0. In addition, the fluorescence response efficiency of bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP (inset a) and bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@NIP (inset b) toward TAP reached the highest at pH 7.0.

The incubation time of bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP and bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@MIP under down/up-conversion excitation channel was discussed in Fig. 3E and F. After the addition of 5.0 μM of TAP, the data of fluorescence response efficiency vs. incubation time showed that the incubation time of bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP (2 min) was shorter than that of the bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@MIP without ZIF-8 doping (4 min), which was attributed to the large surface area of and porosity of ZIF-8 (Liu et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2020).

The down/up-conversion dual-channel fluorescence sensing performance of the sensor was evaluated by the fluorescence response of the bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP and bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@NIP toward TAP with the increasing concentration based on accumulative procedure. Fluorescence quenching efficiency follows the Stern-Volmer equation (F0/F = 1+KSVC, where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of the fluorescence sensor in the absence and presence of TAP. KSV is the fluorescence quenching constant and C is the concentration of TAP). Based on previous works (Fu et al., 2022; Li et al., 2016), the fluorescence quenching mechanism of the bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP sensor was attributed to photoinduced electron transfer rather than fluorescence resonance energy transfer, because of the absence of overlap between the fluorescence emission peak of bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP and UV–vis spectrum of TAP (Fig. S2).

Down-conversion excitation channel for the detection of TAP was shown in Fig. 4 A. With the increase in TAP concentrations, the bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP exhibited the red-shifted emission peak from 456 nm to 480 nm while the unchanged emission peak at 680 nm. The red-shifted was attribute to the addition of TAP interfering with the internal coordination in aCD@ZIF-8, resulting in charge transfer and fluorescence quenching (Vallan et al., 2018; Yin et al., 2019). The fluorescence response of bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@NIP showed similar results (Fig. S3). The slope of fluorescence quenching efficiency ((F456/F680)0/(F456/F680) vs. concentration) was shown in Fig. 4B. The bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP showed two segments with an excellent linear relationship in the TAP concentration range (y = 0.080 C + 0.954 for 5.0 nM- 6.0 μM and y = 0.037 C + 1.205 for 6.0 μM–26.0 μM) with the limit of detection (LOD) (signal-to-noise ratio, 3S/N, where S is the standard deviation of the blank signal and N is the slope of linear calibration.) of 1.9 nM. The sensing performance of bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP was superior to the bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@NIP because the imprinted cavity makes TAP highly accessible to the fluorescence signal. The imprinting factor (IF, IF= Ksv,MIP/Ksv,NIP, ratio of linear fitting slope of bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP and bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@NIP) was calculated as 2.6 (shown in Fig. 4B). In addition, the inset in Fig. 4A showed that the fluorescence color changed from blue to purple to red after TAP was added to bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP under UV irradiation at 365 nm (inset a), which was superior to bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@NIP (inset b), achieving the sensitive and visual multicolor detection of TAP under down-conversion excitation channel.

The coordination-induced emission and signal-amplification effect of the introduction of ZIF-8 were further verified by comparing the fluorescence response of bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@MIP and bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP. As shown in Fig. 4C, the bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@MIP without ZIF-8 doping only showed the emission peak at aCDs (480 nm) and bCDs (680 nm) without the same blue-shifted as bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP, which further indicated the coordination-induced emission of aCDs and ZIF-8. In addition, as shown in Fig. 4D, the bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@MIP also showed two linear responses to TAP (y = 0.02 C + 1.022 and y = 0.012 C + 1.088) due to the imprinting recognition effect (Yang et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2021). The fluorescence quenching efficiency of bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP toward TAP (5.0 nM- 26.0 μM) was higher than that of the bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@MIP. The sensitivity was increased by 4 times, further indicating the combined effect of imprinted materials's high selective imprinting recognition effect and the strong signal-amplification effect of ZIF-8 (Yang et al., 2019).

Up-conversion excitation channel was also studied as synchronous parallel detection of TAP. As shown in Fig. 4E, the bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP showed the triple-emission response at 460 nm, 523 nm and 682 nm toward TAP. Similar results were observed in the bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@NIP (Fig. S4). In addition, the fluorescence quenching efficiency ((F460 × F523/F682)0/(F460 × F523/F682) vs. concentration) was shown in Fig. 4F. The bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP exhibited two segments excellent linear relationship (y = 0.156 C + 0.982 and y = 0.082 C + 1.385) with the LOD of 0.9 nM, and the IF was calculated as 2.1 (Fig. 4F). The fluorescence imprinted sensor demonstrated a parallel dual-channel multi-emission synchronous detection of TAP under down/up-conversion excitation, greatly improving the e reliability of the determination TAP in complex sample.

The sensor's selectivity was evaluated by comparing the dual-channel fluorescence quenching efficiency of bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP toward TAP and four chemical structure analogs. As illustrated in Fig. 5 A and B, the bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP showed a higher quenching efficiency toward the template TAP (add 5.0 μM) than that toward other analogs (CAP, SMZ, SDM and SDZ) under dual-excitation channel (370 nm and 780 nm), along with the visualization of fluorescence color changes with the addition of different analytes (digital image in Fig. 5A). In addition, the linear fitted results of fluorescence response for TAP and each analog within the concentration (5.0 nM- 26.0 μM) were shown in inset in Fig. 5A and B. The corresponding KSV values and chemical structure are listed in Table 1 . For all tested substances, the KSV values of TAP were much higher than those of other analogs under both down/up-conversion excitation channels. The selectivity of the bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP toward TAP was attributed to the formation of TAP-imprinted cavities by hydrogen bonding interaction during the molecular imprinting process. TAP-imprinted cavities complementary shape, size and functional groups exhibited a high selective binding affinity for TAP (Ostovan et al., 2018).

The anti-interference ability of the bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP sensor in complex samples was investigated by a competitive study using potential interfering species (K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Fe3+, Cl−, NO3 −) and four analogs as the coexisting interference substances. The fluorescence detection results based on down/up-conversion dual-excitation channel were shown in Fig. 5C and D. The fluorescence response of bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP toward TAP fluctuated slightly even in the presence of interferers with the same concentrations (5.0 μM). In addition, after adding the bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP, the supernatant separated from the solution containing CAP and TAP (5.0 μM) was detected by UV–Vis. The results showed that the concentration of TAP in the supernatant was lower than that of CAP (Fig. S5 A and B), which may be due to the imprinted cavities of bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP providing more opportunities for TAP to be intercepted (Arabi et al., 2021b; Tian et al., 2021).

In order to further discuss the anti-interference advantages of dual-channel fluorescence detection, the down/up-conversion dual-excitation fluorescence detection of TAP by the bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP in the presence of fluorescence dye was performed. As shown in Fig. 5E and F, fluorescence dyes showed strong down-conversion with no up-conversion fluorescence. In the presence of fluorescence dyes, down-conversion channel fluorescence was significantly affected, while up-conversion channel fluorescence was almost unaffected. Herein, the fluorescence imprinted sensor possessed the anti-interference ability, further revealing the potential of bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP for selective and sensitive parallel dual-channel detection of TAP in actual complex sample.

The application of bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP was evaluated by detecting TAP in the actual fish, shrimp, beef and milk samples through a spiking recovery experiment following the equation (recovery (%) = (CTotal – Csample/Cspiked) × 100, where Csample is TAP concentration in the original actual sample and Cspiked is the standard concentration of TAP, CTotal is the total concentration of TAP after adding a standard spiked concentration to the actual sample). The recoveries in the range of 95.0–105.0% with a low relative standard deviation (RSD) of 4.5% were shown in Table S1, which agreed well with the results of the HPLC-UV analysis as shown in Fig. S6 A-E. The results indicated that the fluorescence imprinted sensor based on dual-excitation channel bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP could supply a well-pleasing platform for accurate detection of TAP in actual samples.

The performance comparison of bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP-based down/up-conversion excitation dual-channel fluorescence sensing approaches with the other previous TAP detection methods, including HPLC and GC-MS method, electrochemical method, UV spectrophotometry method and down-conversion fluorescence method was listed in Table S2. The down/up-conversion dual-excitation fluorescence imprinted sensor displayed the lower LOD (0.9 nM) and broader linear range (5.0 nM- 26.0 μM). In addition, the fluorescence imprinted sensor has the advantages of simple operation, fast response and low cost.

The fluorescence stability was appraised by recording the fluorescence intensity and calculating the RSD of the bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP fluorescence imprinted sensor. As shown in Fig. S7 A and B, the down/up-conversion dual-channel fluorescence intensity was unchanged after continuous UV irradiation at 290 nm for 1 h, revealing the anti-photobleaching property of the sensor. In addition, Fig. S8 A and B showed the photostability of the sensor placed in an environment for 40 days with the RSD of fluorescence intensity change was less than 4.8%. The results displayed the excellent stability of the bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP fluorescence imprinted sensor in an accessible environment.

The repeatability was evaluated by discussing the fluorescence properties of five batches of bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP prepared by the same method. The RSD of fluorescence intensity fluctuation was less than 5%. The reproducibility of the developed bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP was investigated by recording the fluorescence intensity in the five removal-rebinding cycle tests. The fluorescence intensity of each emission peak under down/up-conversion dual-excitation channel was hardly changed with the increase of the reuse, and the RSD of five repetitions was 4.2%, underlining the excellent reusability of bCDs@SiO2@aCDs@ZIF-8@MIP.

A down/up-conversion dual-excitation multi-emission fluorescence imprinted sensor was prepared for synchronous parallel dual-channel multiplexed visual detection of TAP. Dual-mode CDs with stokes and anti-stokes type emission were anchored to the functional carrier to improve the fluorescence sensitivity through the coordination and signal-amplification effect. Dual-channel fluorescence ensured the anti-interference and diversity of data results. The sensor showed the dual-channel response toward TAP with two-part linear ranges of 5.0 nM–6.0 μM and 6.0 μM–26.0 μM, with two detection limits of 1.9 nM for down-channel and 0.9 nM for up-channel. Moreover, the rich fluorescence color changes from blue to purple to red can be observed with the naked eye. The fluorescence imprinted sensor was used to analyze TAP in meat and milk samples with recoveries of 95.0–105.0%, which provided an alternative candidate for sensitive, selective, and multiplexed visual detection of TAP in complex system.

Jinli Fu: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing-Original draft. Shu Zhou: Methodology. Xiaodan Wu: Data Curation. Sisi Tang: Supervision. Pengfei Zhao: Validation. Kangling Tang: Formal analysis.
